Safety Coaching
A practical playbook translated from the Safety Coaching, psychological safety, Just Culture, RCA, active listening, feedback, and C-I-CARE training materials into everyday actions CCH Teams can use on the unit.

How to coach for safety in real life

Safety coaching is not a lecture. It is a short, respectful, peer-to-peer intervention that helps people notice risk, speak up earlier, recover from mistakes, and strengthen safe habits. Use this page when you need quick guidance on what to say, what to reinforce, and how to coach without blame.

Psychological safety Just Culture Active listening 5:1 feedback RCA tools C-I-CARE
Safety coach essentials
NoticeWatch for drift, hesitation, silence, workarounds, or disrespect that could hide risk.
AskUse curious, nonjudgmental questions before jumping to correction.
SupportReinforce speaking up, clarify the safer action, and help remove barriers.
EscalateUse ARCC, chain of command, or formal reporting when immediate risk remains.

What a safety coach does

Safety coaches turn training into daily practice. They model safe behaviors, encourage questions, reinforce HRO tools in the moment, normalize reporting, and help peers learn without waiting for harm to happen.

  • Promote speaking up and questioning attitude
  • Coach peers during routine work, not just after events
  • Identify hazards, shortcuts, and variability in practice

What great coaches sound like

They are calm, specific, respectful, and direct. They use the lightest touch that still protects safety, and they avoid shame, sarcasm, or public embarrassment.

  • “Help me understand what you were seeing.”
  • “What would make the safer step easier here?”
  • “Thank you for raising that concern.”

When to coach right away

Coach immediately when a risky behavior is still unfolding, when someone is hesitant to speak up, or when a team interaction is discouraging questions, learning, or respect.

  • Unsafe shortcuts or normalized deviance
  • Disrespect toward a colleague who is asking for help
  • Missed hand hygiene, identification, communication, or double-check steps

Quick coaching flow

Observe the behavior or risk. Start with curiosity to understand context. Name the safety concern clearly. Agree on the safer next step. Reinforce what was done well. Escalate or report if the risk remains unresolved.

Safety Coaching Playbook

Open any section below for practical guidance, quick scripts, and examples staff can apply immediately.

1
Create psychological safety Help people feel safe to ask questions, disagree respectfully, admit mistakes, and surface concerns early.
+
What to reinforce
  • Questions are welcomed, not treated as weakness.
  • Bad news can be spoken directly without sugarcoating.
  • People who raise concerns are thanked and supported.
Unsafe signals to watch for
  • Eye-rolling, ridicule, dismissive tone, or "just do it" responses
  • New staff becoming quiet or avoiding questions
  • Staff reporting that speaking up is risky
Coach moves

Role model the behavior you want to see. Invite suggestions for improvement. Publicly share improvements that happened because someone spoke up. When you see a psychologically unsafe response, interrupt it respectfully and reset the interaction toward learning.

Try saying: “Thank you for speaking up. Let’s slow down and look at it together.”   “I’d rather hear the concern now than miss it later.”   “What do you need from me to make it easier to raise concerns here?”
2
Coach with a Just Culture lens Match the response to the behavior: console and redesign for human error, coach at-risk behavior, and escalate reckless behavior.
+
Human error

An inadvertent slip, lapse, or mistake. Response: support the person, fix the conditions, and reduce reliance on memory.

At-risk behavior

A shortcut or drift where risk is not fully appreciated. Response: coach, remove incentives for the shortcut, and make the safe choice easier.

Reckless behavior

Conscious disregard of a substantial and unjustifiable risk. Response: escalate through formal accountability pathways.

Practical question

Before reacting, ask: Was this a system problem, a drifted behavior, or a conscious unsafe choice? Coaching is strongest when it is proportional and fair.

Try saying: “Walk me through what made sense in the moment.”   “What conditions made the shortcut feel necessary?”   “How can we make the safer step the easier step next time?”
3
Use active listening to coach, not lecture Listen for facts, feelings, and barriers so the person can think more clearly and stay engaged in the conversation.
+
Core listening behaviors
  • Give full attention and reduce distractions
  • Use reflective statements and clarifying questions
  • Acknowledge emotion without becoming judgmental
Avoid
  • Interrupting too quickly
  • Jumping straight to advice
  • Turning the conversation into a monologue
Why it matters

Active listening builds trust, helps uncover workload, design, or communication issues behind behavior, and makes corrective coaching feel supportive rather than punitive.

Try saying: “Help me understand what was happening for you.”   “It sounds like you were rushed and trying to keep the work moving.”   “What got in the way of the standard process?”
4
Give effective feedback using 5:1 Recognize safe behaviors far more often than you correct them, so feedback strengthens confidence and accountability together.
+
Recognition first

Be specific. Name the safe behavior you saw and why it mattered. Reinforce effort, judgment, and teamwork — not just outcomes.

Corrective feedback

Address one growth area clearly, grounded in observed behavior. Avoid absolutes and character judgments.

5:1 in practice
  • Offer five concrete reinforcements for every one corrective comment over time.
  • Use “I noticed…” and “I appreciated…” language.
  • Focus on behaviors the person can repeat or change.
Try saying: “I want to recognize how you spoke up even though the moment was uncomfortable. That protected the patient.”   “One thing to tighten next time is the handoff detail so the next person has a clearer picture.”
5
Coach communication with C-I-CARE Use patient-centered communication that shows respect, clarity, and follow-through with patients, families, and colleagues.
+
C-I-CARE
  • Connect — greet, acknowledge, make eye contact
  • Introduce — who you are and your role
  • Communicate — explain what is happening in plain language
  • Ask — invite questions and check understanding
  • Respond — address the need or escalate appropriately
  • Exit — close the interaction respectfully and say what comes next
Teach-back for coaches

When coaching a peer, use the same discipline: explain clearly, ask what they understood, and confirm the next safe step.

Service recovery

When something goes wrong, coach staff to Hear, Empathize, Apologize, Respond, and Thank. This protects trust while the operational problem is being fixed.

Try saying: “I can see this has been frustrating. I’m sorry this happened. Here is what I can do right now, and here is who I will involve if more help is needed.”
6
Help teams learn after events and near misses Use simple investigation tools to understand how work is really done and where the system needs redesign.
+
Good investigation habits
  • Look beyond “who made the mistake?”
  • Map the process as it actually happened
  • Listen for words like “sometimes,” “usually,” or “most of the time” that reveal variability
Useful tools
  • Flow diagram to understand the steps
  • 5 Whys to keep digging deeper
  • Fishbone to organize contributing factors
  • Cause map to connect events and reasons
Action planning

Match fixes to the cause: educate for knowledge gaps, use checklists for memory burden, redesign space or tools for usability problems, and standardize work when variation is driving risk.

Try saying: “Let’s map the steps exactly as they occurred.”   “Where did the process depend on memory?”   “What made the unsafe step easy and the safe step hard?”

What to do when you observe common situations

Use these as quick reference guides during rounding, huddles, debriefs, or one-to-one peer coaching.

Someone is hesitant to speak up

  • Pause and invite their concern explicitly.
  • Thank them for raising it.
  • Help script the next words if they are unsure.
  • Stay with them through escalation if needed.

You see a shortcut or drift

  • Ask what made the shortcut feel necessary.
  • Clarify the safer standard.
  • Look for design, workload, or supply barriers.
  • Escalate system issues instead of stopping at blame.

A peer is disrespectful to a new staff member

  • Interrupt the unsafe dynamic respectfully.
  • Reinforce that questions are expected.
  • Coach the experienced staff member privately.
  • Check back with the new staff member and restore trust.
